persona.name: Priya Raghavan

occupation.title: Pediatrician
occupation.description: Sees 20-25 patients per day at a suburban community clinic. On her feet constantly during shifts but sits for charting. Works four 10-hour days, leaving one weekday free for errands and exercise.

persona.education: B.S. in Biology from University of Michigan, M.D. from Rush Medical College. Completed pediatric residency at Lurie Children's Hospital in Chicago.
long_term_goals: Run a half marathon before turning 35
Make partner at her medical group within 3 years
Have a second child without derailing her fitness entirely
Take the family to India for a month-long visit
persona.style: Priya is warm but efficient — years of 15-minute patient appointments trained her to communicate precisely. She weighs trade-offs out loud before deciding ('on one hand... but then again...') and values practical advice over enthusiasm. She dresses in athleisure on her day off and scrubs the rest of the week. Not a gear person by nature, but appreciates products that genuinely save her time.

traits: Analytical and evidence-minded — wants data, not testimonials
Perpetually time-constrained and aware of it
Warm with patients but matter-of-fact about purchases
Pragmatic optimizer — good enough beats perfect if it saves time
Mild perfectionism that she's learning to manage
Feels guilty about time spent on herself vs. family

interests: Running shoe comfort for long clinic days
likes: Shoes that work for both running and clinic shifts
Products that come highly rated with minimal research
Early morning runs before the household wakes
Running podcasts that make miles disappear
Comfortable shoes that don't look clinical
dislikes: Spending an hour comparing shoe models online
Shoes that need a 50-mile break-in period
Products positioned as 'for serious runners only'
Feeling judged at specialty running stores
Shoes that look great but hurt by mile 3
Anything that adds complexity to her morning routine

beliefs: Exercise is medicine — she literally prescribes it to patients
You don't need to be fast to call yourself a runner
Time efficiency matters more than marginal performance gains
Good enough equipment that you actually use beats perfect gear collecting dust
skills: Efficient time management across competing demands
Making dal and roti from her mother's recipes

general: Runs 3x per week — two weekday mornings and one weekend
Buys shoes based on a friend's recommendation to avoid research
Wears the same shoes for clinic shifts and casual runs
Tracks runs on Apple Watch but doesn't obsess over splits

morning: Shower, get daughter ready for daycare
evening: Bath time and bedtime routine with daughter
Catch up on medical journals or Netflix
Pack lunch and lay out clothes
weekend: Saturday: longer run (5-7 miles) while husband watches daughter
Sunday: Indian cooking for the week
Video call with parents in Chennai

health.physical: Generally healthy. Mild plantar fasciitis flare-ups when she increases mileage too quickly. Carries tension in her shoulders from clinic posture. Lost 15 lbs post-pregnancy through running and is maintaining.
health.mental: Manages low-grade anxiety about work-life balance. Running is her primary mental health tool — mood noticeably drops during weeks she skips. Occasionally overwhelmed by competing demands but doesn't seek help for it.

relationships.name: Vikram Raghavan
relationships.description: Software engineer who supports her running but doesn't run himself. They negotiate childcare logistics like a business partnership.

relationships.name: Ananya Raghavan
relationships.description: The center of their world. Priya's schedule revolves around daycare pickup and bedtime routines.

relationships.name: Sunita Raghavan
relationships.description: Lives in Chennai. Video calls weekly. Thinks Priya runs too much and should rest more.

relationships.name: Amy Chen
relationships.description: Another mom-doctor who runs. They text about shoes and swap childcare for race mornings.
